preamble: Labial palatalization, the shift from a voiceless labial stop [p] to a voiceless palatal stop [c], is a cross-linguistically rarer process but well-documented in several language families. This typically occurs when a labial consonant is followed by a high front glide [j] or vowel [i], leading to the loss of labial articulation and the emergence of a palatal stop. This is found in Bantu languages and Southern Italian.

examples.note: Many Bantu languages exhibit palatalization of labials (e.g., in passive formations), where /p/ + /-w-/ shifts to [c].
The Latin cluster /pj/ often develops into a palatal stop, such as [c] (e.g., in Sicilian) or [tʃ] (e.g., in Neapolitan), in Southern Italian varieties.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Schenker, A. M. (1995). The Dawn of Slavic.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Languages: A Historical Introduction. Cambridge University Press.
